basic_info.name: CNT/Graphene Dispersion Solvent
basic_info.description: HSP sphere determination for carbon nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide) enables rational solvent selection for stable dispersions. The HSP of the nanomaterial is determined by dispersing it in a panel of solvents and fitting a Hansen solubility sphere to the good/bad solvent classification. Solvents within the HSP sphere (RED < 1) produce stable dispersions, while those outside yield aggregation and sedimentation. This approach has become the standard method for solvent screening in carbon nanomaterial processing for composites, conductive inks, thin films, and energy storage applications. The method accounts for the balance of van der Waals attraction between graphitic surfaces and solvation by matching solvent-nanomaterial interaction energies.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Carbon nanomaterials possess characteristic HSP values reflecting their surface chemistry: pristine CNTs and graphene are dominated by dispersion forces (dD ~ 17.8 MPa^0.5) with modest polar and hydrogen bonding components (dP ~ 7.0, dH ~ 7.6), while graphene oxide has significantly higher dP and dH due to oxygen-containing functional groups. The HSP sphere is determined experimentally by testing dispersion stability in 30-40 solvents covering Hansen space, then fitting the sphere center (material HSP) and radius R0 using optimization algorithms. A solvent produces a stable dispersion when its HSP falls within the sphere (RED < 1), meaning the solvent-nanomaterial interaction energy is sufficient to overcome the strong van der Waals attraction between graphitic surfaces (pi-pi stacking). The enthalpy of mixing scales with (delta_solvent - delta_nanomaterial)^2, and stable dispersions require this to be minimized. Surface functionalization shifts the sphere center predictably in Hansen space, enabling design of functionalization strategies for target solvents or polymer matrices.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_solv - dD_CNM)^2 + (dP_solv - dP_CNM)^2 + (dH_solv - dH_CNM)^2); RED = Ra/R0; Dispersion concentration correlates with exp(-Ra^2/k)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for stable dispersion; RED < 0.5 for high-concentration dispersion (>0.1 mg/mL); RED > 1.3 poor/no dispersion

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) and R0 of the carbon nanomaterial (from sphere fitting)
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of candidate solvents
Desired dispersion concentration and stability duration
Nanomaterial type and surface functionalization state
data_sources: Experimental dispersion testing in solvent panels (primary method)
HSPiP database for solvent HSP values
Published CNT/graphene HSP values (Bergin 2009, Hernandez 2010, Hansen 2007)
Computational estimation via surface energy approaches
Literature compilations of graphene oxide and rGO HSP values
complementary_parameters: Solvent boiling point and vapor pressure (for processing)
Solvent viscosity (affects dispersion kinetics and sedimentation)
Surface tension of the solvent (Hildebrand approach complement)
Nanomaterial aspect ratio, number of layers, and defect density
Surfactant/dispersant type and concentration if applicable

limitations: HSP sphere for nanomaterials shows significant variability between studies due to differences in material quality, defect density, and functionalization degree
Dispersion stability is kinetic, not purely thermodynamic; HSP predicts thermodynamic affinity but not long-term colloidal stability against reaggregation
The 4:1:1 weighting factor (4 for dD) in the Ra equation was derived for molecular solutions and may not be optimal for nanomaterial surfaces
R0 values for nanomaterials are sample-dependent and vary with preparation method, tube diameter, layer number, and oxidation degree
High surface area and strong pi-pi interactions create energy barriers not fully captured by mean-field HSP approach
Solvent-nanomaterial charge transfer interactions (e.g., with NMP, DMF) contribute beyond what HSP represents
known_failure_modes: NMP and DMF give better dispersions than HSP distance alone predicts, likely due to electron donor-acceptor interactions with the graphitic surface
Water-based dispersions with surfactants where the effective HSP is that of the surfactant-coated nanomaterial, not the bare surface
Solvent mixtures where synergistic cosolvency effects move the effective HSP inside the sphere unexpectedly
Heavily oxidized graphene oxide (GO) where ionic/electrostatic interactions dominate over HSP-type solvation
validation_method: UV-Vis spectroscopy of supernatant after centrifugation (Beer-Lambert quantification)
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for particle size and stability monitoring
Zeta potential measurement for colloidal stability assessment
TEM/SEM imaging of dispersed vs aggregated material

key_references: Bergin, S.D.; Sun, Z.; Rickard, D.; Streich, P.V.; Hamilton, J.P.; Coleman, J.N. 'Multicomponent Solubility Parameters for Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube-Solvent Mixtures', ACS Nano, 2009, 3, 2340-2350. DOI: 10.1021/nn900493u

Hernandez, Y.; Lotya, M.; Rickard, D.; Bergin, S.D.; Coleman, J.N. 'Measurement of Multicomponent Solubility Parameters for Graphene Facilitates Solvent Discovery', Langmuir, 2010, 26, 3208-3213. DOI: 10.1021/la903188a
Hansen, C.M.; Smith, A.L. 'Using Hansen Solubility Parameters to Correlate Solubility of C60 Fullerene in Organic Solvents and in Polymers', Carbon, 2004, 42, 1591-1597
Konios, D.; Stylianakis, M.M.; Stratakis, E.; Kymakis, E. 'Dispersion Behaviour of Graphene Oxide and Reduced Graphene Oxide', J. Colloid Interface Sci., 2014, 430, 108-112
Coleman, J.N. 'Liquid Exfoliation of Defect-Free Graphene', Accounts of Chemical Research, 2013, 46, 14-22
literature.regulatory_relevance: No direct regulatory acceptance of HSP for nanomaterial dispersion. REACH nanomaterial registration requires dispersion stability data but does not specify HSP methodology. EU ECHA guidance on nanomaterial characterization mentions solubility parameters as a physicochemical descriptor.

industry_sectors: Electronics (conductive inks, transparent conductive films, printed electronics)
Energy storage (electrode slurry formulation for batteries and supercapacitors)
Biomedical (graphene-based biosensors and drug delivery carriers)
commercial_tools: HSPiP (sphere fitting module for nanomaterial HSP determination)
Materials Studio (molecular dynamics for nanomaterial-solvent interactions)
Malvern Zetasizer (DLS/zeta potential for dispersion stability validation)
